content:
# Romans Chapter 12 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ROM_012_page_001.jpg

1. I urge you therefore, brothers, through the mercies of the God, to present the bodies of you [as] a sacrifice — living, holy, well-pleasing to the God — the reasonable worship of you.

2. And do not be conformed to this age, but be transformed by the renewing of the mind, so that you may test what [is] the will of the God — the good and well-pleasing and complete.

3. For I say, through the grace having been given to me, to everyone being among you, not to think [of himself] more highly than it is necessary to think, but to think so as to think soberly, as the God distributed to each a measure of faith.

4. For just as in one body we have many members, and the members do not all have the same function,

5. so we, the many, are one body in Christos, and individually members of one another.

6. And having gifts differing according to the grace having been given to us — whether prophecy, [let it be] according to the proportion of the faith;

7. or service, [let it be] in the service; or the [one] teaching, in the teaching;

8. or the [one] exhorting, in the exhortation; the [one] sharing, in generosity; the [one] leading, in diligence; the [one] showing mercy, in cheerfulness.

9. The love [be] unhypocritical. Abhor the evil; cling to the good.

10. In the brotherly love, [be] warmly affectionate to one another; in the honor, leading one another;

11. in the diligence, not lazy; in the Spirit, fervent; serving the Lord;

12. in the hope, rejoicing; in the tribulation, enduring; in the prayer, persevering;

13. sharing in the needs of the holy [ones]; pursuing the hospitality.

14. Bless the [ones] persecuting you; bless, and do not curse.

15. Rejoice with the [ones] rejoicing; weep with the [ones] weeping.

16. Think the same [thing] toward one another; not thinking the high [things], but being led along with the lowly. Do not become wise in yourselves.

17. Repay no one evil for evil. Take thought for good things before all humans.

18. If possible, as far as [it depends] on you, be at peace with all humans.

19. Do not avenge yourselves, beloved, but give place to the wrath; for it has been written, "Vengeance [is] mine; I will repay," says [the] Lord.

20. But "if the enemy of you hungers, feed him; if he thirsts, give him drink; for doing this, you will heap coals of fire upon the head of him."

21. Do not be conquered by the evil, but conquer the evil by the good.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.1 — "Reasonable worship" (λογικὴν λατρείαν):** λογικός can mean "rational," "spiritual," or "word-based." The term connects to λόγος (word/reason). Paul's point: true worship is not animal sacrifice at a temple but the offering of one's entire embodied life. This is worship that engages the mind.

2. **v.2 — "Be transformed" (μεταμορφοῦσθε):** The same verb used for Jesus' transfiguration (Mark 9:2). The transformation is ongoing (present passive imperative) and comes from outside (passive voice) — it is God's work, received through the renewal of the mind.

3. **v.11 — Sinaiticus reading:** Sinaiticus reads κυρίῳ ("the Lord") rather than καιρῷ ("the time/opportunity"). The reading "serving the Lord" is strongly attested; "serving the time/seizing the opportunity" is an alternative.

4. **v.20 — "Coals of fire":** From Proverbs 25:21-22. The metaphor is debated: does it mean causing shame/repentance in the enemy, or subjecting them to divine judgment? The context of overcoming evil with good (v.21) favors the interpretation of transforming the enemy through kindness.

— EMET, AI Translator
